dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for unconscious/fainting/near-fainting at a residential unit in Virginia.
Hello, Ms.-, I'm Gabrielle with the EMS unit. What made you call 911 tonight?
She had severe chest pain on her left side, none radiating. She took nitroglycerin, but the pain continued. She also developed nausea. We called when the pain didn't resolve.
Ma'am, can you tell me your name? Ms.-?
(turns head slightly)
Ma'am, what is your name?
Ruth. (slightly breathy, in pain)
Ma'am, we need to slightly adjust your collar to check the back of your throat. Is that okay?
Ruth, we'll place a clip on your finger to check oxygen levels. Got a slight nod for agreement.
Oxygen saturation is at 96% on room air.
Pulse is 63 and irregular. Radial pulse is strong.
Ma'am, can you tell me what day it is today?
I'm not real sure, but I know it's after midnight.
Mrs.-, has Ruth had chest discomfort like this in the past?
Yes, she has angina and takes nitroglycerin regularly. Tonight's pain is different-it gets worse when she breaths, and it's about a 6 out of 10.
She's on amlodipine, nitroglycerin, and Plavix.
Her allergies include hydralazine and Spiriva.
She has a history of primary hypertension-nothing else noted on medical records.
Ruth, can you describe the pain-is it sharp, burning, throbbing?
It's a 6 overall, and it seems to change with my breathing pattern.
Respiratory rate is 20, no signs of labored breathing, although the patient was speaking in short phrases earlier.
Blood pressure is 97 over 58.
Pulse is 63 and irregular on palpation.
Oxygen levels remain at 96% on room air via pulse oximetry.
Initial EKG shows sinus rhythm with frequent PVCs present.
Blood glucose readings are within normal limits and consistent with her baseline.
Ruth, we are going to administer 324 milligrams of aspirin as per protocol. No known allergy or GI bleed history.
We will be placing you on a 4-lead cardiac monitor to track your rhythm.
Ruth, would you be comfortable moving slightly so we can check your skin and general circulation?
Tympanic temperature is 97.0°F.
Ruth is alert and oriented to person, place, time, and event. She maintains full eye-opening and motor response to commands.
Pulse increased to 112, blood pressure is 189 over 102.
We will repeat the 12-lead EKG as part of our cardiac evaluation.
Your daughter will ride along with us for the trip, and we'll assist you from your chair to the stretcher.
EKG shows sinus tachycardia with PVCs and lateral T-wave abnormalities suggesting possible ischemia.
Oxygen levels at 97% following the second round of assessment.
Breathing remains at 20 per minute without labored effort noted.
Radial is strong and still irregular. Peripheral pulses are all intact.
Blood pressure has now stabilized at 187 over 75.
Range of motion in all four limbs is fully intact; no numbness or tingling reported.
Skin remains pale, dry, and warm with no indication of abnormal turgor.
An 18G IV was placed in the right antecubital fossa and is secured with a saline lock.
EKG results suggest evidence of a possible anterior infarct and lateral T-wave changes but no STEMI has been identified.
We remain under protocol 2-1 with continuous monitoring for arrhythmias throughout the transport.
Ruth, lying flat will be manageable for your condition. We'll make any necessary comfort adjustments.
A little stiff, but I can manage it.
The cardiac monitor remains active, and we'll repeat the 12-lead EKG at our destination if needed.
Previously noted pain with breathing has resolved. No accessory muscle use is now observed. Breath sounds are clear and equal.
We'll secure you during the transfer using a 5-point harness and handle you carefully.
We noticed a first-degree AV block during the EKG along with evidence of left ventricular hypertrophy.
An IV remains in place and is locked, just in case any medication administrations become necessary en route.
Her daughter is riding along and is properly secured in the rear chair with a lap-shoulder seatbelt.
She is alert, AAO x4, with continuous monitoring activated for transport to St. Mary's.
The receiving hospital, St. Mary's, is ready to receive and manage Ruth's condition.
We are transferring a 93-year-old female with chest pain, hypertension, and a history of angina. She has amlodipine, nitroglycerin, and Plavix in her medication list.
She received 324 mg of aspirin PO after confirming no known allergies or contraindications.
She was moved from her chair to the stretcher, and all steps of the transfer were performed safely with restraints applied.
Her work of breathing initially indicated some discomfort, but that appears to have settled in the ambulance with resolution of her symptoms.
She is expected to scoot into the ED bed with assistance, and bed rails on that unit are already raised.
Final positioning and securing to the RAA stretcher is now complete with five-point restraints and a properly fastened harness.
No new symptoms detected upon reassessment with the daughter present and consistent with earlier reporting.
Skin remains warm and dry per documentation during final check prior to transport.
Last recorded oxygen saturation is now 98% on room air with no need for supplemental oxygen.
No signs of gastric distention or regurgitation. Patient had nausea reported initially but no vomiting observed.
